title: Lesson 8: Ordinal Numbers & Derivative Verbs   ترتیبي اعداد او مشتق کریاوې
description: Expand your vocabulary exponentially by mastering Derivative Verbs—the most productive morphological class in Pashto. You will also learn ordinal numbers and explore authentic Afghan food culture.
duration: Textbook pp. 113–135 (approx. 10-15 pages of primary instruction plus Workbook exercises) [cite: 40, 43]

content.heading: Module 1: Ordinal Numbers (1st - 10th)
lines: Welcome to Unit 8! Let's begin with sequencing events using Ordinal Numbers (first through tenth)[cite: 42].
Ordinals are formed by adding the suffix [-am] (ام) to the cardinal numbers[cite: 42].
Notice that in Pashto script, numbers are written using Arabic-Indic numerals, but we use Western numerals in our English explanations[cite: 43].

content.text: Cultural Mnemonic: A great way to remember ordinal numbers is through the Islamic daily prayer sequence (first prayer, second prayer, etc.), which naturally orders the day in Afghan culture[cite: 42].

content.heading: Module 2: The Derivative Verb System
lines: This unit introduces the most productive way to build your vocabulary: Derivative Verbs[cite: 42].
You can turn almost any adjective or noun into a verb by combining it with a 'light verb'[cite: 42]. There are two paths:

content.heading: Transitive vs. Intransitive Examples
lines: Transitive (Making it happen): لیلا کور تودوي. [Layliā kor tōd kawalay.] (Layla makes the house warm.) [cite: 42]
Intransitive (It happens naturally): کور تود شوی. [Kor tōd shawəy.] (The house became warm — note the perfective aspect here.) [cite: 42]
Commands: تیار یې کړه! [tayār yē kawa!] (Make it ready!) [cite: 42]

content.text: Phonetics & Grammar Hack: For derivative verbs, the stress falls on the final syllable of the base word, not the light verb[cite: 43]. Also, pay close attention to the intransitive perfective change: the [sh] sound in [kedəl] becomes an explicit [sh] perfective marker (shaway)[cite: 43].

content.question: If you want to say that you are actively cleaning a room (making it clean), which light verb should you combine with the adjective 'pāk' (clean)?
content.explanation: To express an active, transitive action (making something clean), you must use the light verb 'kawəl' (to do/make). Using 'kedəl' would mean the room is becoming clean on its own[cite: 42].

content.heading: Module 3: Afghan Cuisine & Recipes
lines: Our reading for this unit immerses you in Afghan food culture[cite: 42]. You will read a recipe entirely in Pashto script!
We will be studying the traditional 'Char Masala' spice blend, a staple in Qandahari cooking[cite: 42].

content.text: Cultural Context: Afghan meals are highly communal affairs[cite: 42]. The centrality of bread ([nān] نان) cannot be overstated—it is used to scoop food and is deeply respected[cite: 42]. Additionally, offering tea ([chai] چای) is the bedrock of Afghan hospitality; it is always served to guests before and after meals[cite: 42].

content.question: In the context of the Afghan recipe reading, how do you correctly form the ordinal number 'third' using the standard suffix?
content.explanation: Ordinal numbers are formed by adding the suffix '-am' to the cardinal number[cite: 42]. Therefore, 'dre' (three) becomes 'dre-yam' (third)[cite: 42].
